topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the lab felt like a spotlight, exposing every flicker of anxiety in Elara’s chest. The results were printed on glossy paper, stark lines of genetic code that suddenly felt like a verdict. Her fingers trembled as she traced the names—names that belonged to a family she had never known. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. She stared at the screen, the vibrant colors of the DNA markers swimming before her eyes, a dizzying kaleidoscope of unfamiliar history. When her guardian finally walked in, Elara could only manage a small, choked sound, her gaze fixed firmly on the floor, unwilling to meet the eyes that now seemed to hold the weight of this revelation.

[story 2]

Liam sat on the edge of his bed, the phone screen illuminating the dampness on his cheeks. The confirmation had landed like a physical blow. He reread the report for the tenth time, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A strange, hollow ache spread through his limbs, making him want to curl into himself. He pressed his palms against his eyes, trying to physically block out the intricate tapestry of shared ancestry displayed on the screen. When his sister called, his voice came out thin and reedy, a brittle sound that didn't quite carry the shock he was trying so desperately to contain. He just wanted the noise to stop, the evidence to vanish.

[story 3]

Maya stood by the window, the afternoon sun too bright, too sharp. The DNA report lay on the table, a folded piece of paper that seemed impossibly heavy. Her jaw was clenched so tightly that a dull throb resonated behind her molars. Every sound in the apartment—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed amplified, pressing in on her. She walked to the kitchen, her movements slow and deliberate, as if navigating a minefield. When she finally looked at the names, a sudden wave of profound disorientation washed over her; the world tilted slightly, and she had to grip the counter to steady herself, fighting the urge to simply turn and flee the room.
